impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's determination that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems not covered by the patent), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense. This decision reinforces that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to compel purchases of unpatented goods with independent commercial uses.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF benefit from clearer limits on patent holders' ability to leverage patent rights into adjacent product markets, promoting fair competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk if they bundle patent licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for unpatented products, even where the products are related to the patented technology.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions using emulators and models of function calls are directed to abstract ideas under step one of the Alice framework for patent eligibility under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment against Norton (Gen Digital), holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions via emulation and function-call comparison are abstract at step one of Alice. The court remanded for further proceedings on step two. The decision also reversed the district court's denial of JMOL on damages from foreign sales and vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent infringement suits involving software-based detection methods gain a stronger basis for challenging such patents as abstract under § 101, potentially reducing exposure to large damages verdicts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with cybersecurity and software-related patents face heightened invalidity risk under § 101, potentially diminishing the value of significant patent portfolios in the computer security space.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5 million damages award in favor of GoTV, holding that claims directed to server-based tailoring of content specifications for wireless devices are patent-ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas despite the district court's pre-trial ruling upholding eligibility. This decision required entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies benefit from stronger § 101 defenses against patents claiming routine content adaptation for different device capabilities, reducing patent troll exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face increased risk that their patents will be invalidated as abstract under § 101, even after surviving pre-trial challenges.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (which had been granted against Tenaris) and Walker Process antitrust fraud (which had been granted in Tenaris's favor), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves coiled tubing patents where the patentee allegedly failed to disclose prior art CYMAX documents acquired from a predecessor company. The decision sends both the patent enforceability and antitrust claims back for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence on genuine factual inquiry rather than summary disposition; competitors pursuing Walker Process claims get a chance to prove antitrust fraud at trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face continued exposure to both unenforceability and antitrust liability when there are factual questions about whether material prior art was withheld during prosecution, creating significant litigation risk and cost.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it missed the deadline for seeking a stay of the original declaratory judgment action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) because BioMarin had missed the statutory deadline in the original action. The court refused to allow strategic refiling to circumvent the statute's time limits, preserving the district court's ability to adjudicate Ascendis's safe harbor defense under 35 U.S.C. § 271(e)(1) while the ITC proceeding continued.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing ITC proceedings retain the ability to pursue timely-filed declaratory judgment actions in district court without facing gamesmanship through refiling tactics designed to trigger mandatory stays.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders pursuing parallel ITC and district court litigation cannot reset mandatory stay deadlines by dismissing and refiling declaratory judgment actions, limiting their ability to control the pace of parallel proceedings.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding specific AAV capsid proteins are patent-ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 as directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to engineered host cells containing recombinant AAV sequences are not directed to a natural phenomenon under § 101. The court found the claimed cultured host cells are man-made products with no natural counterpart, distinguishing them from naturally occurring substances. This is a significant win for the gene therapy industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotechnology and gene therapy companies benefit from clearer protection for engineered biological constructs under patent law, supporting investment in gene therapy platforms and AAV-based treatments.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the gene therapy space face renewed patent infringement exposure as claims to engineered host cells and vectors survive § 101 challenges, potentially increasing licensing costs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft in a patent infringement case involving network optimization technology. The court found the district court improperly excluded the expert's analysis, which cascaded into denying discovery reopening and granting judgment on absence of remedy. This decision reinforces that district courts must carefully evaluate damages methodology before excluding expert testimony entirely.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a higher bar for excluding damages experts, ensuring they have the opportunity to present reasonable royalty theories to a jury rather than losing cases on summary judgment due to expert exclusion.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft face reduced ability to obtain early case dispositions through Daubert challenges to damages experts, potentially increasing litigation costs and exposure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether PTO Director instructions governing the Board's exercise of discretionary authority to decline IPR institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the Board on when to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from formal notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate non-institution authority, and the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the Director's broad discretion over IPR institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued broad discretion to decline IPR institution, which can protect patents from duplicative or harassing challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR to challenge patents face continued uncertainty as the Director's institution guidelines remain insulated from both judicial review on the merits and procedural APA challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement functionality infringed Masimo's patents and properly issued a limited exclusion order barring their importation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches incorporating blood oxygen measurement technology covered by Masimo's patents, and upheld the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing models. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This is a major decision affecting Apple's smartwatch product line.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders pursuing ITC exclusion orders against major importers receive strong appellate backing for enforcement of technology patents through import bans, creating powerful leverage in licensing negotiations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies like Apple face significant product exclusion risk at the ITC, potentially forcing product redesigns or licensing agreements to maintain access to the U.S. market for key product features.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, finding claims directed to web conferencing with time-shifting and asynchronous playback capabilities failed to state a claim. The decision adds to the growing body of Federal Circuit precedent invalidating software-related patents under § 101, reinforcing the high bar for patentability of web-based collaboration and streaming technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Google benefit from continued narrowing of enforceable software patent claims, reducing exposure to infringement suits from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the web conferencing and collaboration technology space face additional § 101 vulnerability, reducing the commercial value of such patent portfolios.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement and erred in granting JMOL for the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed DePuy's JMOL victory, finding the district court abused its discretion in excluding both of the patent holder's expert witnesses on induced infringement of spinal surgery device patents. The case is remanded for a new trial at which both experts may testify. This decision is significant for medical device patent litigation, reinforcing that expert exclusion must be carefully justified.
impacts.positiveImplications: Medical device patent holders benefit from a more rigorous appellate standard for expert exclusion, preserving their ability to present technical evidence of induced infringement by device manufacturers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies like DePuy face renewed trial exposure and potential damages liability when appellate courts reverse expert exclusions, increasing the cost and uncertainty of defending induced infringement claims.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by charging PACER fees to fund non-PACER expenses. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, noting that hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed. The decision validates the settlement after nearly eight years of litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users will receive reimbursement for overcharges, and the settlement establishes limits on the judiciary's ability to use access fees for unrelated purposes going forward.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claims reciting 'control means' for raising and lowering a crop harvester header are indefinite when the specification describes the corresponding structure in general terms without detailed algorithms.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester height control patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification's description of the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation was sufficiently definite. The court found that the specification adequately disclosed the controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system as corresponding structure. This revives patent infringement claims against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more forgiving approach to indefiniteness, particularly where the specification describes corresponding structure at a general level common in the relevant field.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment and other manufacturers face renewed infringement exposure when means-plus-function claims survive indefiniteness challenges, potentially requiring licensing or design-arounds.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a money-mandating obligation sufficient to establish Tucker Act jurisdiction, and whether the SBA's failure to award grants in the order applications were received states a viable claim for damages.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants stated viable Tucker Act claims against the United States for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that the RRF statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in the order received) created a money-mandating obligation, giving the Court of Federal Claims jurisdiction. The SBA allegedly awarded grants out of order, processing later applications before earlier ones, depriving plaintiffs of grants before the fund was exhausted.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grants or benefits under mandatory statutory schemes have a viable path to recover damages when agencies fail to follow statutory distribution requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potentially significant damages liability when agencies deviate from statutory mandates in distributing appropriated funds, creating fiscal exposure across similar grant programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly found no violation of Section 337 as to active respondents for failure to prove likelihood of confusion or dilution of 3D trade dress marks, and whether the Commission properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed as untimely Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents and affirmed the Commission's entry of a limited (rather than general) exclusion order against defaulting respondents for Crocs's 3D trademark marks on its Classic Clog shoes. The decision limits Crocs's ability to obtain broader import protection for its iconic shoe design through the ITC.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and competitors benefit from the ITC's high bar for proving trade dress infringement and dilution, and from limits on obtaining broad general exclusion orders based solely on defaulting respondents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking ITC protection for 3D product trade dress face difficulty obtaining general exclusion orders and must timely appeal no-violation findings or lose the right to appellate review entirely.
